title: "Working at Microsoft is a well-compensated exercise in navigating complexity"
review:
Brand name still carries weight. It opens doors, even if internally most doors lead to another meeting.
Compensation is solid enough to keep you from asking too many difficult questions.
Flexible work arrangements exist, which is useful when you need time to do actual productive work outside the meeting calendar.
Smart people do exist. You just have to find them between layers of process, tooling, and alignment calls.
Access to enterprise customers and large-scale problems, at least in theory. Metrics often replace meaning. If it can be tracked in a dashboard, it matters. If it helps the customer but can’t be easily measured, it’s optional.
Heavy reliance on internal narratives. “Transformation” and “AI-first” tend to mean repackaging existing problems with better slide decks.
Meetings are the default operating system. Work is something you try to fit in around them.
Ownership is diffuse. When things go wrong, there’s usually a team, a process, or a framework to point at instead of a person to fix it.
Feedback loops are weak. Issues are acknowledged, logged, and then quietly preserved for future consistency.
Internal competition and role overlap can make simple outcomes unnecessarily complicated.

title: "Money must compensate for lack of mission, poor leadership, and culture"
review:
Meta previously offered top-tier compensation, although this has started to only apply to favorites or the highest performers. Additional RSUs are provided every year.

While many benefits were impacted by the Year of Efficiency, the company retains some good benefits (parental leave, recharge, PTO, free food).

There are good people at the company who are technically excellent and of high character. Certain teams have been able to create a healthy comraderie and counter-culture, despite the continued degradation of the overall company culture and direction. The company currently suffers from a lack of mission and purpose, as well as conviction around its direction. I think this ultimately stems from Mark, who cares more at this stage about having a share in the next fad, instead of focusing on how the company can uniquely contribute and serve its users. 

The culture of the company has deteriorated significantly since 2022. There is not much empathy shown to employees, and decisions from leadership are made in haphazard ways that often lead to organizational thrash (for example, overzealous hiring, then layoffs). With the advent of TBD and MSL, Mark is now treating Meta as a new company centered around these teams, but supported by the old monetization systems created for Facebook. 

This old world-new world dynamic suggests a lack of trust from leadership towards Meta's old guard, and contributes to a feeling of hierarchy and lack of transparency/openness. The current company operates as a directed graph where information flows towards TBD, but without information flowing back outward.

As with any large company, there's a mix of good and bad people, both in terms of technical talent and character. This contributes to the politicking and scope fighting that occurs within the company.

title: "Unempathetic senior management, good for career ramp "
review: Amazon experience opens up lot of opportunities in your career Unempathetic senior management, comp structure is inhumane, employees are taken advantage of with promo carrot

title: "Good for career growth/comp "
review: Lots of opportunities to grow and learn. Great engineers to work with Very team dependent and leadership dependent. Your experience will vary greatly depending on your manager

title: "Can be good, but politics is everything"
review:
Good pay with RSUs depending on your start date
Can be interesting work Everything is dependent on your team, org, and director level politics

title: "Both good and bad"
review: Learning a lot within short period of time, easy to switch between roles Work-life balance and bureaucracy. Toxic management if you are unlucky. Too many re-orgs.

title: "Fair WLB "
review: Looks good on resume, accelerate career growth . Can have good autonomy in some teams . Terrible culture and WLB,can destroy your mental health sometimes .

title: "Not as bad as made out to be"
review: Pay, interesting work, problems to solve, some good folks around. Coworkers can vary a lot. Many lookout for only themselves

title: "Great wlb and pay until ai "
review: High compensation, unmatched anywhere else. Hard to find a better paying job Extremely tight schedule with ai fluff. Too many tpm spilling ai bs

title: "Great start for early career but team dependent"
review: Great kick off for your cs career. You will learn a lot of aws stuff and have access to ai tools. Changes can happen a lot. And when changes come, it might not always be smooth.

title: "Fear based work culture leads to slow innovation and less collaboration"
review: Decent pay, but poor leadership/management. Poor support for new AI innovation, projects/pressure only come top-down. Lacks that energy The culture of stack ranking and pips means people don't collaborate, even in person, because they are scared. A culture driven by fear means devs take less risks because a bad move means you're cut. Less risk means less innovation in the company. All new tooling is from top down push, not bottom up, so there is a lack of start up culture. This culture directly leads to slow-growth and fear based performance, which is not good for AI innovation or spontaneity for new groundbreaking ideas. Feels restrictive and slow.

title: "Experience varies from team to team"
review: Good pay, impact is big, good place to start your career wlb can be improved, no benefit at all, culture similar to other big tech

title: "Can build good products and services for sense of accomplishment but you are out if you take it easy even for a bit"
review: Good products and services - PMs can really lead hence the good products No wlb and now very toxic culture. Long hours and no recognition

title: "Doesn’t follow what it preaches"
review:
wlb depending on the team
decent pay
brand value on resume bureaucratic promo process
frequent layoffs
career growth is next to zero

title: "Was a great learning ground. Gone downhill with recent external VP hires"
review: Good training ground. Learning to build and operate at scale Politics. Comp model. Too many layers of reviews. Unable to move fast because of size of company.

title: "Fast growth and ownership, but chaotic priorities, constant rework, and uneven WLB depending on org."
review: You’ll likely own end-to-end work, not just small pieces. It’s one of the few places where work actually gets deployed and used at scale. You learn how to operate under pressure, deal with messy real-world data, and align with multiple stakeholders. The brand still carries weight and opens doors externally. Priorities change all the time, and you’ll redo work more than you’d like. A lot of “high impact” work turns into fire drills or reactive tasks. Scope is broad but sometimes shallow — you may not go very deep technically unless you’re in a specialized team. Performance and promotion are very manager-dependent, and expectations aren’t always clearly defined. WLB is inconsistent, and it’s easy to burn out if you don’t set boundaries.

title: "Horrible place to work."
review: Great people to work with. That is the only pro about working there. The CEO’s are clowns. Company is toxic. They can’t keep promises. They change the rules every day. It’s not worth the stress of knowing if you’ll have a job tomorrow.

title: "Not Impresses "
review: It’s fine. I like my org but I think it’s really team dependent. So much process. There are good and bad teams so do your research.

title: "Not great"
review: Compensation is okay for high performers, and some teams have surprisingly good work-life balance The minute you get hit with your first reorg, career growth stops and never recovers. The company culture also does not seem to value its employees – no perks even when they would cost little to the company, stack ranking and poor compensation updates, etc.

title: "Day 2"
review: Good comp, stock finally doing well. Can do interesting work if you can find it Poor leadership now; lack of growth for senior leaders. Day 2 mentality

title: "Great learning, poor work life balance "
review: Great learning, good compensation and you will be working with all the new technologies. No work life balance, corporate politics and bureaucracy. Heavy internal competition for growth not worth it.

title: "Good team but manager protects himself over team. Experience changes based on team to team. "
review: Decent pay and this team has a good work life balance. May work 40-45 hrs a week. Lot of them. Politics, team mates who need to show they are better than you, no kindness.

title: "Team was great"
review: Good pay, nice oncall bonus, looks good on resume and future career opportunities Long working hours, five days in office, lots of politics and pip factory

title: "Great growth opportunities, overworking and hierarchical culture"
review: Room to grow and get a lot done. Tech driven Normal and rewarded to overwork, seattle centric culture, non personal management

title: "Bad culture toxic teammmates"
review: Good pay in the first year and cash bonus. Stocks are also good. Free bananas Teammates are not willing to help and toxic. Backstabbing is quite common among peers

title: "Day 2 sadly now"
review: If you like working 16 hours a day every week, it’s kinda lit It sucks if you’re in the 99% of people who don’t sell their soul to their company

title: "Good learnings"
review: If you want to be best in the business, amazon will push you to the edge and more. So if you want to learn and grow, this is a good place. Don’t have hopes on work life balance. You may get it in a few teams, but mostly you won’t.

title: "Not as bad as the reputation"
review: I had a great manager, compensation is very competitive for the area RTO nightmare, rolling layoffs, not enough exposure to varying tasks

title: "Good compensation, Poor culture "
review:
Good compensation 
Good technical oriented personal 
A place with lot of experience and experienced personal Way to much bureaucracy it feels like the company is built from process and from delivery.
Very hard and rigid culture

title: "No scope for promos"
review: Lots of intelligent people and opportunities and interesting work. Huge scale. L8+ leadership is largely incompetent and spineless across the board. Bake offs between teams creates an unhealthy working environment. Very uneven promotion / performance ratings. Cultural favoritism. Lots of scope hoarding and empire building. Andy Jassy.

title: "Helpful for career growth "
review: Wells defined LPs to make decisions or to convince teams. Work life balance can be further improved through better management

title: "Toxic culture in most of the teams"
review: There are some exceptionally good teams, Depend on where you work Ugly competition, Leadership encourage killing each other and work with fear.

title: "Terrible work life balance, everyone thinks they are a main character "
review: Great compensation, generally pretty free to work on what you feel is important. Absolutely awful culture. People are regularly fired with zero notice for made up or trivial reasons. Multiple people on my team had this happen to them. It destroys team morale. Nearly all of the engineers think they are the main character of the company and only know the Right Way to do something. Be careful not to question someone with even mild seniority, all it takes for you to get immediately fired is a single complaint to their skip, and they know it. Theres no collaboration between teams at all, so theres multiple solutions to the same problem all over the codebase.

title: "Amazing place to work, learn, and have impact"
review: The compensation is excellent, and the work is genuinely at the forefront of technology. You get to collaborate with exceptionally talented people, with plenty of opportunities to grow and take ownership. In my org, there is a strong sense of trust and very little micromanagement. The company also takes time off seriously and actively encourages people to use it. Work-life balance can be mixed. My org tends to be better than some others, but it is not unusual for people to work late.

title: "Awesome colleagues; high pressure, no WLB"
review:
Awesome tech, smart folks, interesting problems to solve.

Definitely will catch up to Anthropic in Enterprise if will just focus on that. Constantly changing direction; vibe management, folks could be gone one day.

title: "Needs better WLB, "
review:
- Smart, impressive people
- Growing organization
- Intellectually simulating - Work Life Balance
-  Lack of direction
- Opaque structure
- High pressure and low support

title: "Rare combo of small company feeling and high impact"
review:
- Extremely smart, capable, and hard working colleagues 
- Impact is through the roof. AI is chatgpt to many.
- valuation is going to continue to grow so everyone's stock based comp will keep climbing - Still trying to figure out the right structure of a growing company, which means it can feel pretty chaotic at times.

title: "Amazing"
review: Top of industry talent and exciting projects to work on Lot of work. Work life balance can be pretty bad

title: "Exciting Work"
review: OpenAI is a very heads down company. Few meetings, little to no micromanaging, and lots of autonomy. If you’re doing what you are supposed to be doing people rarely bother you. For good and for bad. There is almost no feedback outside of the review cycles so you have to just use your best judgement to feel how you’re doing

title: "I love it here but not for everyone"
review:
Lots of hard problems to solve
Tons of smart ppl to learn from
Strong culture and in-office vibes
Everyone is happy to be here
Always feels like contributing good value. some ppl are annoyingly entitled and whiny about always asking for more and not compromising. Entitlement can leak into work sometimes. 

Long hours but it’s fun with the people around you

title: "Great place for driven people"
review:
Food is free and imo delicious
Very dynamic and fast paced environment 
Lots of interesting work Wlb but at least for my team, you’re not forced to stay late, mainly everyone wants to work late. Not a problem at all if you’re a driven individual.

title: "Top of market comp"
review: Great place to work if you are used to high paced startup environments and AI excites you Lots of changes due to hyper amount of growth/growing pains

title: "Inexperienced mgmt including CXOs "
review:
They pay well 
Brand opens doors 
Good to have on your CV Politics. Greenhorn entitled CXOs & VPs including Brad Lightcap who was no relevant experience to boast of and was made COO because he knows and sucks up to Sam. These people feel they deserve all this - no humility at all. Very mediocre inexperienced bench at the top. VP product partnerships and VP media partnerships are other losers. These guys shouldn’t be titled above Director. 

Nepotism in the CMO org. Everyone is ex Coinbase :-) 

Lot of laid off mediocre folks from Meta with inflated titles across teams and orgs

title: "Exceed expectation "
review:
Smart and kind people
lots of interesting projects
good perks
lots of visibility and transparency in leadership Because the pay is already top of the market, no growth for promotion or increases in salary

title: "Great overall experience"
review:
Company with very bright future. Constantly willing to improve and does not get too comfortable riding on the coattails of its past and current achievements. You get the pros of working at a large company with some pros of working at a startup.

Talented colleagues, good management, good culture. Genuine feedback is welcomed and taken into consideration by management. WLB is reasonable given the pace at which we move. Better than the horror stories I heard before joining this company. Not a con for me. But if you want to find a place to drop down and do nothing while collecting a pay check don't join. If you are low ambition don't join

title: "Busy"
review:
Talented coworkers
Interesting product and problems
Company moves fast 
Hot product Company moves fast
Culture is still sorting itself out
Stressful

title: "Not for me"
review:
- Excellent pay
- People may feel you are smart - Dead end job
- Stress
- No WLB
- No idea what to do the next

title: "Mileage may vary"
review:
-comp is great

-benefits and perks are good

-great talent density - lots of industry legends taking steps/scopes down to build -disjointed review / promotion process, very difficult to get promoted even as a high performer (though working here is great for your overall career)

-WLB can vary wildly, with some rarely seeing the light of day (though these folks are highly compensated)

title: "Bad WLB, sloppy craft, interesting domain"
review: The hype, the paper money, the people, the bleeding edge The hype. Everything is urgent, so everything is sloppy and incomplete.

title: "terrible wlb "
review: mission critical work; smartest coworkers in the world; room for growth if you’re willing to put in the time genuinely crazy wlb; people are too busy to help and mentor. i also got low balled coming here

title: "Poor WLB but extremely cool opportunity to make an impact "
review: I love my team and the projects are very cool Company is scrappy and lots of broken systems / processes. Very fast paced and change constantly

title: "Great comp, doable WLB, highlight of my career "
review: Engaging work, brilliant coworkers, very little bureaucracy, very good upside for comp It’s not hard to keep work life boundaries but you’re definitely advantaged to work mode

title: "Tough work life balance but a fun place to work"
review:
Everyone around you is super smart and capable
at least on Infra I haven't seen too much politics
You get to work with upcoming cool tech, even if you're not in research its cool being part of an exciting company WLB can be non existant
Things change around so often

title: "A lot of money but extremely overworked "
review: Comp is very high, high career growth, brand name is strong Very very long hours, a lot of politics, not a fan of management

title: "Great so far"
review:
Ownership
Smart people around
Great componensation
Hockey stick growth after 300b valuation Not really sure how things change.
Requirements are up in the air

title: "Toxic and political culture"
review:
Smart co-worker
High compensation 
Good brand for resume
Some team are pretty good Terrible culture, inside openai is extremely toxic and political. High turnover once a new leader comes onboard, they quickly wash out original team and bring their own ppl. Then that inner circle start to pointing fingers everywhere. 

Ppl get fired for a reason or simply because your new leader doesn’t like you, folks get promoted last months and fired this month for low performance. No expectations from leadership because they know nothing about your work but they say it as: performance bar is very high and so as expectation. 

No respect to employees as a person unless you are in research. If you get some complicated thing done well, no appreciation. If you happen to make a mistake, you are fired. If you are not in the inner circle of the leader, and their ppl make a mistake, you are fired too because their own folks’ only job here is to point fingers.

Most ppl didn’t make to their one year and leave with no stock in hand, openai being playing this cheap labor game really well.

title: "Extreme room for growth, and great comp philosophy"
review:
Even if work is easy (grunt work), high volume work rewarded (i.e. comp and project goals are aligned).
But there’s also tons of room for personal growth

Management is vocal about ensuring we don’t burn out; and taking time off for ourselves Lots of passionate people who (voluntarily) work a lot -> you may feel pressured to work more than you’d like

title: "openai is nothing without its people"
review:
* talented, dedicated, and mission-driven people. Especially on the research side. 
* OpenAI is a very undermanaged company and so lots of exciting things bubble up.
* in-person culture (con for some, I like it)
* get to be on the bleeding-edge of research. * feels like every week, there's another crisis that management pivots to. This churn can be a lot and put a lot of people on edge. Management feels very inexperienced, I've had some bad managers here
* Not great work life balance
* coordinating between many teams/orgs at a large company is taxing

title: "Intense, rewarding, high talent density"
review: Team is generally talented and mission-driven. Plenty of interesting projects to be found. Intense, certainly a lot of competition in the space. Going through growing pains that most startups face in terms of structure and culture.

title: "a true rocketship with tons of room to keep going"
review: leading company at the frontier of a new technology cycle — fast growth means plenty of broken processes, information gaps, etc but nothing unexpected! things can be hectic at times and coordination is more challenging as overall org size has grow

title: "Pinnacle of tech"
review: Super meaningful work, plenty of scope to make everyone happy, all co-workers are exceptional, set up to win, super fast stock growth avoids conversations around career progress and levels and everyone just focuses on doing their best work, executives moving quick and are thinking really big Can get exhausting at times, but leadership has understanding and encourages rest. Food quality doesn’t live up to the rest of the company.

title: "Not the same as before"
review:
If you land in a good team you can have an easy job. For many roles, the expectations are blurred, and as long as you are doing something, you'll be fine. * Recent growth resulted in hiring a lot of people, which, combined with generic interview questions, dropped the bar. Any decent FAANG SWE can now pass the interview at +1 level. The only trick is getting the interview.
* Recent military contracts and now getting a huge investment from trump are dropping the original ethics level the company was trying to uphold. 
* The growth is happening without a clear charter, teams hire because they were told to not because they have a problem to solve. This happened in Google in 2019 and will ultimately result in layoffs I think.

title: "You’ll make good money but you may end up over/underworked"
review:
Cushy 
Pays well
Ton of time to learn new stuff Can be really slow 
Management is really detached from actual work

title: "Sinking ship"
review:
Great before 2023
Good culture once 
Known brand and good market share Worst company ever after 2023
Too many bad Executives hired

title: "Good WLB & Bad setup"
review:
Good work and reasonable timelines.
Good support from management for selective folks. Unless you are friends with management, you won't get good work, hampering your career and growth. Management won't let you know that while you are working there. You will be almost pampered irrespective of the work as long as you are delivering, but outside market won't be caring much about work you do at this legacy company.

title: "Not so great place to work!"
review: Get to work on different FinTech products (rewards, BNPL, CBT, seller protection etc) instead of basic payment infra (checkout and P2P) Uncertainty about layoffs! Every month there will be some set of layoffs happening somewhere

title: "Large fintech with strong impact at scale, but can feel slow and process-heavy."
review:

* Work on large-scale, real money systems
* Generally good work-life balance
* Mature engineering practices & infra
* Internal mobility across teams * Team experience can vary significantly depending on manager and org
* Some teams may have high expectations with limited support or alignment
* Internal politics and favoritism can influence growth and opportunities
* In certain cases, employees may feel a need to over-deliver to gain visibility and trust

title: "Dying company with no path forward"
review: Free cabs, decent Comp, good work life balance in places Bad stock, lacklustre management and hiring very bad talent nowadays

Overall Compensation is good

title: "Work life balance is good to a certain extent. Hh"

title: "Good work-life balance, bad leadership management."
review: Good work-life balance. My managers are good as well colleagues are friendly. Very, very poor leadership from top-down. Low total compensation, especially with the stock price drop.

title: "Great to kickstart career "

title: "Overall okay"

review: Work culture, perks, solving large Fintech challenges at scale, WLB Currently, growth has slowed which has led to a big stock dip and quick changes to leadership. Need to climb back up aggressively this year

title: "Great WLB"

Great WLB I never saw anyone work past 5pm
Pay is decent Lack of tech. Still struggling to get in the cloud

title: "Work life balance depends on the org and team you’re on. My team is basically on call 24/7 so.."
review: Lenient in-office regulations, very supportive and friendly people, great people to learn from very political, management is terrible, no work structure (within my org at least), food is really mid

title: "Good package. Better wlb"
review: Polite people. KPI driven. People help each other. Overall good work life balance. Company is direction less. Leadership is clueless. Lots of ad hoc changes.

title: "Great Work life balance "
review: The company work culture is good with great Work life balance compensation is slightly lower when compared to other big companies

Sometime you can get good manager so you can learn and grow fast. Office politics

review:
PayPal is great. Good projects and people in teams. Good approach PayPal needs work life balance, and pay is not that great I guess . I don’t see any other cons

title: "Becoming chaotic"

Good pay, you don’t have to work for the most part Run by extremely stupid people and nothing gets done
 Word word

title: "Bad work life balance "

title: "Terrible WLB and bad leadership "
review:
Engineering bar is good, opportunities to learn on distributed systems at scale. Credit stealing amongst team mates because of ERD/ Diff counts (really bad way to measure engineer productivity)
Pathetic leadership with no spine (Indian managers especially they will say yes to impose deadlines and escalations and then backstab during perf reviews, I hope they rot in hell)

title: "Okay work"

title: "Good pay and learning opportunities, but work-life balance can be challenging."
review:
Good compensation and benefits compared to many companies.
Opportunities to learn new skills and work on large-scale systems. Work-life balance is not great and can feel stressful at times.
Management decisions can change quickly and cause uncertainty.
Career growth can feel slow depending on the team and role.

title: "Great company and peers, bad on-call"
review: Team mates, compensation, on-site culture, tech stack, tools and equipments On-call, timezones, leadership, role expectations and constant contexg switching across projects

title: "Got horrible quick"
review: Comp above average. Opportunity to grow. Market still values the brand... But soon will be treated like garbage like Amazon Culture is rotten. Never been as stressed in my life. Got a new autoimmune disease and aged badly

title: "Terrible place to work for"
review:
Free food, decent pay, great brand name!
Nothing else to add here - Impossible level of politics. Any initiative is killed by the team and culture.
- No one helps anyone else. Very generous in blaming others.
- Everyone is in a no holds barred brutal fight for promotions and raises
- Actual engineering is non-existent, but there do exist great relics from the past
- A lot of internal platforms are just promotion-driven slopjobs, making life difficult for everyone else.

title: "Great benefits"
review: Interesting projects with a lot of opportunities for growth and development Work life balance does not exist and there’s constant pressure

title: "People are good, management is very short sighted"
review:
Good perks and compensation at all levels 
Smart people to work with Lots of metrics tracking 
Lots of team politics and favouritism

title: "High volume, low impact "
review: The food is ok. As of today, you can work from anywhere for 1 month. High volume, high stress, big ego, try-hard hustle culture. Fear-based leadership trickles all the way down. People aren't more productive; they're just scared and make up work. Whatever Anthropic does to succeed, Uber is the opposite. The product is no longer cutting-edge. Rather Uber partners with companies that are actually doing interesting work. I can't believe I spent 4 years of my life doing high-stress, but boring work.

title: "Good benefits"
review: Benefits are good, wlb is team dependent. Hybrid model, free food. Work might be mundane. Felt company is bloated and might be reduced in coming times.

title: "Okayish"
review: There is Flexibility of work with great colleagues. Not great projects though. There’s a constant fear of layoffs with too much politics.

title: "Great company to work for"
review:
Lots of good problems to solve
Still fast growing company
Awesome coworkers Work life balance is not satisfactory, especially during outage mitigation

title: "Good comp, whether you like or not depends on team"

title: "Growth & Hussle"

The culture encourages ownership and collaboration, which supports professional growth. However, priorities can shift quickly, and work-life balance may vary depending on the team. Overall, it’s a solid place to build skills, though it requires adaptability.

title: "Not great "
review: Benefits and pay are solid for the industry. Office amenities are nice. Bad culture, incompetent management, aggressive styles are rewarded. Experienced a lot of harassment.

title: "Terrible culture, terrible WLB, terrible engineers"

Culture is toxic, inhumane WLB, and the engineering bar has gone down drastically in the last few years. If you’re looking to actually learn how to be an engineer, I would recommend not joining a taxi company.

title: "Decent "

title: "Good Benefits"

title: "Stellar Executives Subpar everything else"

title: "Good pay for non-tech, bad WLB"

title: "Smart & interesting company"

A lot of times are interactions with customers seem transactional as opposed to strategic and that backfires when you’re trying to expand a customers wallet share after the transaction. To be a data company a lot of the data is all over the place and there’s always hesitance amongst solution engineers to actually jump into the product to showcase what we have to offer and I don’t think it’s their fault. I think the company is not providing tools and they enablement necessary to demo within the product.

title: "Going in wrong direction"
review:
At this point its becoming harder and harder to find any. This used to be a great place to work, but things have shifted over the past 1–2 years. Work is now heavily metrics driven: tracking check-ins, work items cycle times, reviews, etc. and management only focuses on those numbers. Perf rating are close to auto generated using these.
Few releases back they introduced  “advanced planning” process essentially reintroduced a waterfall approach under a new name. Trust is really low in general, micro management is increasing everyday. Even smallest things are dictated to people with 20+ years of experience. Also, too many approvals and too much paperwork are needed to get anything done. 

Behaviors have changed to match incentives. Tasks like answering customer queries or helping other teams aren’t measured, so they happen less unless someone wants to network. Meetings reward visible participation, so people speak up just to be seen even when they do not have anything to add, wasting everyone's time. 
Escalations used to be rare, today we did three and got one, because approaching a release freeze this is the only way to get other teams to talk. Also, middle managers use to gain visibility...

title: "Big Name, Big Stagnation. Stay for WLB, Leave for Sanity and Growth."
review:
Big name on the resume. IBM still turns heads in some enterprise circles. It carries weight for future roles if you can spin the experience right.

Scale is real. You're dealing with massive enterprise systems, data at planetary levels. Progress moves in months/years, not sprints, which can feel glacial but is the nature of the beast.

Work-life balance in pockets. Bureaucracy and corporate inertia mean some teams (mine included) coast without insane pressure. Flexible-ish time structuring (though gated by your manager), and the apathy actually helps avoid burnout in the short term. This place is a soul-crushing mix of apathy, fear, and systemic rot that makes real work impossible.

Constant fear and instability from endless layoffs/rebalancing. Every quarter brings whispers (or announcements) of cuts. Entire teams vanish overnight, projects stall mid-stream, conversations die in committees. Morale is in the gutter—people are just surviving, not thriving. Recent rounds (thousands gone in Q4 2025/early 2026) hit hard, and it's not "performance-based", it's arbitrary rebalancing to chase AI/software margins. You never know if you're next.

Apathy and indifference everywhere. Burnout and disengagement are the norm. People phone it in because why invest when the rug could get pulled? WLB is "good" only because no one cares enough to push hard. Apathy masquerading as balance.

Isolation and zero culture. No real team spirit, no collaboration energy. You're siloed in tiny groups (my team is 3 people), remote/hybrid feels lonely, and there's no social fabric left after years of cuts and offshoring.

Bizarre, dysfunctional management setup. You report to a manager you barely work with directly, or worse, one who's an IBM lifer who's built an unbreakable moat around himself. Knowledge hoarding is rampant, lifers guard internal apps/scripts like crown jewels because sharing risks their job security in the next chopping block. No real ownership for anyone below them; you're basically an assistant pushing PRs while they hold the keys. Systemic inefficiency baked in: business units stay unstructured chaos, no growth, no fixes.

Career stagnation trap. Legacy/internal proprietary tools mean zero transferable skills. Resume bullets after a year? "Maintained internal apps under constraints." No client impact, no modern stack exposure, no real technical depth or leadership. It's career suicide if you're mid-career and want growth, skills atrophy while you navigate stand-ups (thanks to timezone-forced global teams) with an unpredictable manager.

Fear-driven hoarding and micromanaging. Everyone's terrified of the next layoff, so knowledge isn't shared, innovation dies, and managers cling to control. Projects fail by the wayside because nothing moves without endless approvals, and the fear culture kills any momentum.

Advice to interviewees/candidates: If you're A JUNIOR, new grad, and/or risk-averse and just need a steady paycheck + big-name cred for a couple years, it might work (some teams coast fine). But if you value ownership, growth, modern tools, or basic job security—run. The "rebalancing" never stops, the lifers never leave, and the apathy is contagious. I've seen too many sharp people get stuck here and regret it.
Would I recommend to a friend? No, unless they're okay with stagnation and uncertainty.

Our principal devs and architects are incredibly knowledgeable, patient and happy to mentor when you show interest. Tools and processes are antiquated and torturously slow (both to launch and run). It can take business days to push your code, and it's an arduous process to patch and test your feature. I also feel like I'm not advancing my skills since I'm using ancient technology.
As with other tech companies, layoffs have been happening frequently. So even with having a great manager and having great reviews, raises + promotions + bonuses are rare and even when given, quite small.

title: "A Corpse of What it Was"
review:
Being paid is nice but that’s where the pros stop. Your job responsibilities will change at the whims of your management with little you can do about it. You might be hired for one reason but then made to do something outside of that skill set. When you don’t succeed it’s on you for not having the skills (despite it not being what you were hired for)

There is zero trust in the company and for good reason. I’ve consistently seen where managers and above will have an employee do something ill advised(directly deleting an entry in a live production database for example) and then throw them under the bus for it when it goes wrong. 

Work/life balance went out the windows years ago. If you are brave enough to set boundaries you are treated as a pariah. 

Pay is under market for most software positions (at least in network) and yearly pay increases/bonuses are strictly controlled not by merit but by % of people reaching a particular bracket. Too many over performers? Well only one of them gets the bonus they should and the others get the minimum. 

Everything is a fire drill all the time. Management doesn’t plan ahead so there is a new emergency every couple of days.

title: "A very middling company "
review:
Normally it has a solid work life balance - things used to run slow here because of all the red tape you need to go through to get things approved. But I’ve recently worked 55+ hrs/week for 4 weeks so I may change my answer if this keeps up. 

About 80% of people there are nice and caring. The company pushes for RTO and currently don’t hire any remote workers (and have been laying remote workers off lately). 

Pretty large HQ in Bellevue. Campus provides complimentary lunch. The 20% are aggressive and strongly-opinionated assholes. If you are one though, it does get you far here. 

Career growth can get stagnant. It depends on the team you’re on. My team didn’t have promotions for 3+ years but a recent re-org and push has gotten a quarterly promotion cycle established. A lot of opportunities to switch teams if you get bored.

review:
There is work life balance and benefits are not bad. Hierarchical, top-down decision making. Siloed work. Protecting your turf mentality. Zero collaboration. 100% dis-trustful environment, back biting, no accountability, no growth opportunity, ICs treated as slaves, middle management are spies for VPs, and if they find someone questing why things are done the way they are, they report you to the VP.

politics politics politics

Business does not trust IT, IT bosses around and bullies their way into solutions.
No one is leading, everyone is just waiting for the ship to sink. More than 70% employees are 10+ yrs exp, no young blood from top to bottom. Old world trying to survive in the new competitive environment.

Strategy is good but absolutely no execution on it. It is only a matter of time; this house of cards will collapse.

title: "New Grad / Early Career stayed here for almost 3 years"
review:
I joined through a new grad program. It was a great place to learn and grow, but it could have been just my specific team. I learned some cutting-edge technology that helped me a lot with my career such as Snowflake, Databricks, Apache Spark, Microsoft Azure. My specific team was very kind, collaborative, and there were a lot of events that were fun, and people i met that helped me out a lot and learned from But on the flipside, there was a migration so there were some really legacy not so great work and technologies that were a nightmare to work with. But working with that gave me understanding of technical architecture, technology debt, and the tradeoffs of decisions. 

I think some parts are of the company are very political, and certain teams, are not onboarding friendly so i've heard some bad stories about joining and not being able to integrate. My very first team actually was like that so if i never switched it would have been a horrible experience. As in not much room or space to contribute, not being able to work on projects, and managers getting frustrated but not training

title: "What I’ve learned"
review: Discounts, good product, service, & training is great. Customer Service was top notch when in the US only due to being direct employees and having the ability to fully assist the customer on the first call. I hear FCC may mandate that back from the telecom companies. Recent micromanagement culture that wasn’t a thing in the past. Makes doing your job harder with too many internal meetings. Let Sales people sell. Stop asking about progress of the sale 3x a day. It’s only necessary maybe 2x a week max. Let us work, we’ll bring in the opportunities. Telecom in general think outside selling is still relevant today but most buyers work from home. They need to take success notes from the tech sector. In person door knocking is no longer a huge impact to the bottom line. It actually slows down the amount of sales that are possible in a day. Some of the leadership are stuck on processes in the past that used to work but have a negative impact now.

title: "Legacy leadership is the real problem (hiring from Amazon is making it worse)"
review:
Company is excellent at attracting experienced talent from respected, complex organizations.
	•	Big problems to solve; real opportunity to modernize and build at scale.
	•	Many smart, hardworking people who genuinely want to improve the company.
	•	Strong brand and market momentum. MongoDB is very good at recruiting experienced operators from large, sophisticated organizations. People come in excited, ready to apply what they’ve learned at scale, and eager to raise the bar.

What many of them run into, however, is a deeply entrenched layer of long-tenured employees who wield influence without corresponding accountability or capability. Progress often depends less on the quality of ideas and more on navigating internal alliances, personalities, and unwritten power structures.

New leaders quickly discover that driving change requires either winning over stakeholders who may feel threatened, or finding ways around them. When expectations are clarified or standards are raised, routine management often gets reframed as “escalation.”

The result is predictable: strong hires spend more time managing politics than delivering outcomes. Momentum slows, frustration builds, and many eventually decide their energy is better spent elsewhere.

There are talented, well-meaning people across the company. But until incentives shift toward ownership, urgency, and measurable impact — rather than tenure and internal positioning — MongoDB will continue to struggle to fully capitalize on the very talent it works so hard to recruit.

Like most places, I think it is team dependent, and I like my team. Being remote is great. There is some push for local RTO but it seems lax. Company is growing but not over hiring like so many companies do. Meaning there’s less turnover and less layoffs. I feel that my job is mostly secure. Especially coming from a FAANG adjacent company. We have a lot of engs in Beijing. There is a culture and language barrier with many of them. But the biggest con is there are late meetings. I also have seen an increase in red tape, ie meetings, writing reports/summaries. Which has been annoying. Pay is decent. But the yearly bonus does not quite make up for the lack of equity.

title: "Run."
review:
Okay-ish comp for better-than-average talent. Not competitive for top candidates - Clueless leadership. Prioritizing superficial things while treating engineers like a waste. There is nobody left to maintain the existing services. At the same time, leadership thinks that engineers quitting is a good thing because they can replace them with AI. That doesn't actually happen. All the burden is eaten up by ICs while the leadership celebrates "AI cost savings".
- Majority of top talent already left. The remainder is looking to leave soon. People only stayed here because of appreciated grants. The company was kind enough to release the golden handcuffs by switching from 4-year to 1-year grants at the end of 2022 so it doesn't apply to most people.
- Nobody cares about engineering culture, quality, or reliability. PMs vibe code AI slop. Short-term gain is more important than anything else.
- Infrastructure at this company simply doesn't work.

title: "Interesting place to work, spent 6 years there"
review:
Free food in office
Work at Robinhood which is fun in regards to product
Generally good people It’s one medium sized party and you’re probably not invited

Company is extremely slanted towards product/eng organizational focus

There is a secret club of employees that Vlad supposedly selects that get extra bonus/vacation/event invites 

The company refuses to promote internal employees, asks employees to perform at a higher level then says there is not a “business need” for a promotion

Company claims to reward high performance but actually encourages mediocrity. Perf rating scale is abysmal at 3 ratings, distribution is 20% 3’s, 70% 2’s and 10% 1’s. 

Everything is about driving profits from the users trading, even when it’s clearly bad for the customer, to get execs phat stock payouts, what else is new in finance companies I guess. It’s just gross coming off of the company’s original mission and products.

title: "Fast-paced fintech with real product impact, but demanding culture isn't for everyone"
review: Shipping velocity is genuinely impressive — you can go from idea to millions of users in weeks, not quarters. The product scope has expanded a lot (prediction markets, futures, banking), so there's always something new to own. Equity compensation is competitive and the mission still resonates with the team. Smart colleagues across the board, especially on the eng side. Work-life balance is basically nonexistent during major launches. Priorities shift fast — sometimes mid-sprint — and context-switching is constant. Headcount is intentionally lean so you end up covering a lot of ground that would normally be split across 2-3 PMs at other companies. Upward feedback loops to leadership can feel limited.

title: "Balancing Innovation in an AI Driven World"
review:
Pros
- Strong benefits package that supports employee well-being  
- Scheduled company shutdowns that encourage rest and prevent burnout  
- Employee Stock Purchase Program (ESPP) offers opportunities for long-term investment  
- Clear team differentiation that can highlight impact and accountability Cons
- Struggles to stay relevant in an increasingly AI-driven and rapidly evolving landscape  
- Rigid hierarchical structure that can limit creativity and slow decision-making  
- Stock-based compensation (RSUs) has declined in value due to stock performance  
- Lower participation in stock programs, even among high performers  
- Perceived favoritism and inconsistent reward structures across teams  
- Team differentiation can at times reward underperformance while penalizing high-performing groups

review: Full independence to work on things and learning curve is also great Full of SAP world so finding role outside SAP world is difficult.

title: "Great diversity, inclusion and work life balance. Global presence "
review: You learn a lot about business in and across industries. Great customer base. Career growth and comp not great. Leadership is horrible lately. Great old culture of trust and respect is completely gone.

title: "Overall good"
review: Work life balance is great. Decent and supportive managers. Benefits in Canada are great TC could be better. Not a clear way of how to get promoted to the next level, seems everything depends on a team budget

title: "Toxic management"
review: Good place for networking and to spend a few years terrible to stay long term. There’s absolute dinosaurs around who’ve been here over 20 years and are not going to change their ways despite how much the company strategy evolves. Management is made up of these dinosaurs!!

title: "Great to grow yourself "
review:
1. Working with Apple to learn the logic and how to make things done 
2. Bag company with complete rules 1. Can’t be work and life balance due to overloading job as factory runs 24/7
2. Cost always comes the first so the employees benefits are not as good as people thoughts

title: "Don’t expect anything"
review: Scope of work is big so new graduates have opportunity to learn. High turnover rate because of extremely long working hours and low compensation package and minimum employee benefit. Lack of work-life balance. No career growth due to unclear policy and favoritism.

title: "No expectations No disappointment"
review:
Compensation and benefit is barely OK.
A good platform for graduates. Office hour is way too long. Not flexible at all

title: "Bad compensation and work life balance"
review: Good platform to develop skill sets in the industry if you have the right role and teams and customer teams to work with No boundaries from work and personal life. Very bad and outdated company structure. Employees get bad treatment.

title: "Don't expect high"
review: Good for new grads and those who needs sponsorship on Visa. Very low and non-negotiatable compensation. No career growth. Horrible management.

title: "Not the worst"
review: Good for new grad without good offer. Depends on the team, might actually learn a lot of things. Most of RD work is fake due to toxic environment and poor management.

title: "It really depends (manager and division)"
review: Your manager could be giving you a lot of flexibility. Low pay. no raise. Poor Sr. management. No procedure so you can be learning a lot and creating your own

title: "Terrible compensation "
review: Good work life balance. Work load is manageable with more than expected flexibility. Terrible compensation with minimal chance of improving over time. Company structure is inefficient.

title: "Terrible place"
review: Really no pros for this, thinking to leave this company from the 1 week since I joined Terrible culture. Terrible people to work with. Terrible pay.

title: "Don't come here if you have other opportunities"
review: Low bar, not much work for sde. A great story to tell your kids about how you survived your most miserable time. Low compensation, poor management, zero career growth, toxic culture. These company actually forces employees to work in office even some teammates get COVID-19.

title: "Lack of direction and bureaucracy upheld by archaic management "
review:
Ok benefits (health, 401K) but minuscule vacation. Some leaders/managers are fantastic people who try to instill a positive work culture despite top-down management’s lack of direction. Work life balance is non-existent - you will be working Taiwan hours so be prepared to not be able to schedule anything personal on weekdays and occasionally weekends. Processes and systems are all over the place and management does not hold toxic people or non-performers accountable. The culture is still very much “old-guard” and while there’s some emphasis on change it’s nowhere near the level required. 
Foxconn needs to benchmark against other technology companies and begin matching compensation, benefits, career development and especially culture.

title: "Poor management and compensation"
review: Only fair work life balance and that is all the pro Poor compensation even for full time employees who’s software engineer

title: "Good place to start"
review: Well known company. A good place to start your career. No growth. Low comp. No learning opportunity. No work life balance.

title: "Good company to start your career "
review: You work closely with Apple team so you can learn a lot from that. If you can’t join apple as a new grad or you don’t have any other choice then join Foxconn becomes a good idea. Super low compensation. No career growth. Company expected you to leave after years working here.

title: "Depends on location you work at, review can be huge different"
review: Opportunity for those who really want to climb ‘ladder’ ,  good internal transfer opportunity Tech company but manufacturing comp structure.  Everything need to get through HQ’s approval

title: "hard work but good pay"
review: lots of work and stressed out coworkers, but at-least pay is good lots of work, people steal scope, growth is org dependant

title: "Enjoy working here"
review: Lots to learn, ownership is extreme you get to influence systems more visibly. Competence is respected You have to continuously push boundaries learn new stuff and grow your influence for maintaining performance

title: "Good experience so far"
review: Good career growth. Great tech to work on. You will get to own things real quick. benefits can be good and the rest is all good, too much work can be tiring.

title: "Toxic environment "
review: Brand name helps your resume and you can learn a lot in this fast paced environment Some teams are extremely toxic. Pressure from the high up leads to stress and unprofessional manner.

title: "Fun work"
review: Good problems to solve, lot of talented people to work with. Lack of work life balance, amenities and equity refreshers seem to be limited.

title: "Great company products"
review: Great to work on company products and modern tech stack Lot of products feel like a startup, so you will have to work more than 9-5

title: "Really fulfilling if you're in the right team. "
review:
Smart and Kind team Members 
Decent benefits 
Learning opportunities 
Helpful with immigration Low balls on pay
Low balls on leveling 
Slow Promotions

title: "Loads to learn, but lots to sacrifice"
review: Incredibly talented colleagues. Fast paced. High-impact. Lots of opportunity to learn. Can be very political. Work life balance can be challenging.

title: "Decent experience in Fremont 4680 cell team "
review: WLB in cell team is better than rest of Tesla. Good start of you're a fresh grad and willing to grind High work stress, low job security, low base, bad parking, no hybrid, constant pivots in company direction. Tough to be in management. Juggling life events and work is impossible. Many staff engineers and senior managers are actually poor performers.

title: "A lot of work and opportunities for growth"
review: Opportunity to grow if you are prepared to grind. Good pay and total comp growth. Very unstable job security and terrible work life balance. Management experience is luck of the draw, but there are some good managers out there.

title: "Don’t come here unless it’s autopilot team; if you are general IT department, consider this is tie 3 company"
review: Bad wlb will make you accept new offer without hesitation. Anything here you can think of is downside; one big issue is, there is not enough toilet stalls for men.

title: "Too high of an attrition, leaders not on the same page"
review:
Your only hope to enjoy the work is if people alongside you are fun to work with as Managers lack influence, leadership skills and intent to impact your career positively. Hence, it is highly team and org dependent. Vehicle power electronics has a bad reputation across teams due to frequent new product initiatives with unrealistic timelines since the beginning. Most staff level people have got there due to a retention offer owing to high attrition on their team. More often than they are the reason for others leaving. 

No perks at all hence the pay package is awful for HCOL area.

title: "Good work life balance based on your team. I worked at Engineering Design division at Palo Alto so it was good for me."
review:
Great place to learn
Can interact with members from different teams easily Cafe food is over priced 
If you work in Palo Alto, the rent in the neighborhood is pricey

title: "Run by incompetent managers "
review:
Stonks when its up, nice headquarter, cool products, nothing more Toxic coworkers, toxic bosses.
The only way to great promoted is to suck up to the manager.

title: "Grindy culture"
review:
Meaningful work
Lean teams
Amazing product space
Passionate teammates
Shipping fast No work life balance
Bad benefits
Potentially low TC
Fear of layoffs

title: "Great growth"
review: Fast paced and meaningful ownership . Tech and communication skills grow significantly. No life outside of work. Especially when products deadlines approaching

title: "Horrible boss, thrown out like trash after a burning out quarter "
review: Salary, health insurance, most coworkers are smart and hard working After reorg I got a horrible boss who always suppress my ideas and growth. Tons of politics and the performance is not based on positivity results, but based on supervisors biased judgement. Had years of torture and finally found out supervisor trying to screw up me using ugly approaches for a long time. At the end being thrown out like trash, what a horrible experience

title: "Place sucks"
review: A good amount (but dwindling) of passionate people with incredible talent. Moves very fast for a large corporate Most people suck especially management who are wildly incompetent or just yes men. Culture makes employees feel demoralized from day one

title: "Whether you like this place or not is highly team dependent "
review: Lots of new tech, bleeding edge of stuff, fast paced Toxic management and work life balance, lots of people at the top have no clue of the work being done

title: "cool"
review: love working here, IT is not as bad as everyone says. always interesting work in IT infra. compensation is good you are expected to work a lot. but this is known

title: "Ok"
review: Growth opportunity is limited to certain groups and teams in the business Lack of work life balance makes people to get exhausted

title: "Strong solid company to work for "
review:
Software Development 
Not a 5, but a good company overall. 
Easy growth from P1 Associate SWE up to P3 Senior SWE, and not very hard P4 promotions if you are decent. A P5 promotion to Senior Staff is harder and depends on the org as well, example IT org has many P5s - likely lower equity, so tactics to retain talent

Good impactful projects to work on. All orga have some exciting product to be exposed to.

Sponsor immigrants in a semi automated way

Competitive compensation above market 

WFH not so bad if you have reputation and strong leader PTO (Paid Time Off) is a joke. Unlimited is a lie. Some orgs force 15 days a year, and that makes no sense

Do not trust HR. They are here to help Tesla, not you.

Many orgs with micro management weak leaders

Unclear career growth checklist beyond P4

Company growth uncertainty beyond 2026 - just risky bets on risky businesses

title: "Worst Culture but Great Minds"
review:
Constantly challenging which helps to improve yourself. Most of the team members are good. It will take forever but worst work life balance.
If you are in manufacturing area, worst work life balance * 10000 
Poor management

compared to my last 2 internships

Large scale
Good exposure to products that very few companies have

A lot of things seem to be already done, most new projects are objectively very stretched nice-to-haves, so it’s a lot of “supporting” long-term goals that have already been executed
